text: The Grand Poem Exchange. Vermia has decorated the room with small worm drawings on bits of paper strung between the bookshelves. It somehow looks genuinely lovely.

speaker: Vermia
text: OKAY. Rules: you read your poem, the person it's about cannot respond until everyone has finished. Then we have tea and talk. Ready?

speaker: Grubwin
text: Where did you get tea?

speaker: Vermia
text: I brought a kettle from home. It's in the closet. I've been keeping it here for three months.

speaker: Grubwin
text: You've had a kettle in the club closet for three months and none of us knew.

speaker: Vermia
text: It was for a special occasion! This counts.
Dirtus enters, slides under his arm, slightly windswept.

speaker: Dirtus
text: I wrote four poems. One for each person. I couldn't narrow it down.

speaker: Mawla
text: The exercise was one poem.

speaker: Dirtus
text: I had four observations.

speaker: Vermia
text: We'll hear all four. Okay — I'll go first.

Vermia's poem is for Mawla. It's about stillness. About how some people are like the deepest soil layer — you can't see what they're holding but you know it's everything. Mawla listens with her eyes forward. She doesn't move.

Grubwin's poem is for Vermia. It's about belief — not whether Scatha is real, but about what it means to need something to be real. It's careful and kind and it makes Vermia press her hand to her mouth.
Dirtus reads four poems. They range from surprisingly moving (Vermia's) to entirely about how Grubwin's clipboard is a metaphor for emotional distance (accurate, apparently) to yours, which is—

speaker: Dirtus
text: Yours is about potential. About someone who showed up not knowing what they were walking into and had the right kind of open eyes. I think Scatha sent you here specifically.

speaker: [player]
text: ...Thank you, Dirtus.

speaker: Dirtus
text: You're welcome. Also I've added you to the summoning roster. Hope that's okay.

speaker: Grubwin
text: There's a summoning roster.

speaker: Dirtus
text: Slide seven.
Then it is your turn. Everyone is quiet.
You wrote your poem about one person this week. You knew from the moment the assignment was given. You hold the paper and you read it.
